entry.title: Bayesian Linear Regression
entry.content:
The Linear Regression Model

A normal linear regression model assumes an output variable YYY, a vector of covariates x=(x1,⋯ ,xp){\bf x}=(x_1,\cdots ,x_p)x=(x1​,⋯,xp​), and YYY is linear deterministic function βTx\bf \beta^TxβTx with additive Gaussian noise. <|–more–>That is

Y=βTx+ϵ,ϵ∼N(0,σ2).Y={\bf \beta^Tx}+\epsilon, \epsilon\sim \mathcal N(0,\sigma^2). Y=βTx+ϵ,ϵ∼N(0,σ2).

Given nnn i.i.d. samples (x1,y1),⋯ ,(xn,yn)({\bf x_1},y_1),\cdots, ({\bf x_n},y_n)(x1​,y1​),⋯,(xn​,yn​), the joint probability density of observed data y1,⋯ ,yny_1,\cdots, y_ny1​,⋯,yn​ conditional upon x1,⋯ ,xn\bf x_1,\cdots,x_nx1​,⋯,xn​ and the value of β\bf \betaβ and σ2\sigma^2σ2 (or, the likelihood function) is

p(y1,⋯ ,yn∣x1,⋯ ,xn,β,σ2)=∏i=1np(yi∣xi,β,σ2)=(2πσ2)−n/2exp⁡{−12σ2∑i=1n(yi−βTxi)2}.\begin{aligned} &p(y_1,\cdots,y_n|{\bf x_1,\cdots,x_n},\beta,\sigma^2)=\prod_{i=1}^np(y_i|{\bf x_i},\beta,\sigma^2)\\ &=(2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2}\exp\{-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta^T{\bf x_i})^2\}. \end{aligned} ​p(y1​,⋯,yn​∣x1​,⋯,xn​,β,σ2)=i=1∏n​p(yi​∣xi​,β,σ2)=(2πσ2)−n/2exp{−2σ21​i=1∑n​(yi​−βTxi​)2}.​

Maximizing the likelihood function is equivalent to minimizing the sum of squared residuals SSR(β)=∑i=1n(yi−βTxi)2SSR(\beta)=\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta^T{\bf x_i})^2SSR(β)=∑i=1n​(yi​−βTxi​)2. Let X=[x1,⋯ ,xn]T\bf X=[\bf x_1,\cdots,x_n]^TX=[x1​,⋯,xn​]T and y=(y1,⋯ ,yn)T{\bf y}=(y_1,\cdots, y_n)^Ty=(y1​,⋯,yn​)T. We have

SSR(β)=(y−Xβ)T(y−Xβ)=∥y−Xβ∥22.SSR(\beta)=(\bf y-X\beta)^T(y-X\beta)=\|y-X\beta\|_2^2. SSR(β)=(y−Xβ)T(y−Xβ)=∥y−Xβ∥22​.

The minimizer, also called the ordinary least square (OLS) estimate, is given in the closed form (suppose XTX\bf X^TXXTX is invertible)

β^ols=(XTX)−1XTy.\hat{\beta}_{ols}=\bf (X^TX)^{-1}X^Ty. β^​ols​=(XTX)−1XTy.

The closed form can be obtained using linear algebra, which is often explained as the [geometric meaning of least square](http://users.isr.ist.utl.pt/~wurmd/Livros/school/Bishop - Pattern Recognition And Machine Learning - Springer 2006.pdf).

Bayesian Linear Regression

A Semiconjugate Prior for β\betaβ

Suppose β∼N(β0,Σ0)\beta\sim\mathcal N(\beta_0,\Sigma_0)β∼N(β0​,Σ0​). The posterior distribution for β\betaβ is

p(β∣y,X,σ2)∝p(y∣X,β,σ2)p(β)∝exp⁡{−12(−2βTXTy/σ2+βTXTXβ/σ2−2βTΣ0−1β0+βTΣ0−1β)}=exp⁡{βT(Σ0−1β0+XTy/σ2)−12βT(Σ0−1+XTX/σ2)β}.\begin{aligned} &p(\beta|{\bf y,X,\sigma^2})\propto p({\bf y}|{\bf X,\beta,\sigma^2})p(\beta)\\ \propto& \exp\{\bf -\frac{1}{2}(-2\beta^TX^Ty/\sigma^2+\beta^TX^TX\beta/\sigma^2-2\beta^T\Sigma_0^{-1}\beta_0+\beta^T\Sigma_0^{-1}\beta)\}\\ =&\exp\{\bf \beta^T(\Sigma_0^{-1}\beta_0+X^Ty/\sigma^2)-\frac{1}{2}\beta^T(\Sigma_0^{-1}+X^TX/\sigma^2)\beta\}. \end{aligned} ∝=​p(β∣y,X,σ2)∝p(y∣X,β,σ2)p(β)exp{−21​(−2βTXTy/σ2+βTXTXβ/σ2−2βTΣ0−1​β0​+βTΣ0−1​β)}exp{βT(Σ0−1​β0​+XTy/σ2)−21​βT(Σ0−1​+XTX/σ2)β}.​

Therefore, the posterior distribution for β\betaβ is Gaussian with

E[β∣y,X,σ2]=(Σ0−1+XTX/σ2)−1(Σ0−1β0+XTy/σ2)Var[β∣y,X,σ2]=(Σ0−1+XTX/σ2)−1.\begin{aligned} &\mathbb E[{\bf \beta|y,X,\sigma^2}]=(\Sigma_0^{-1}+{\bf X^TX/\sigma^2})^{-1}(\Sigma_0^{-1}\beta_0+{\bf X^Ty/\sigma^2})\\ &\textrm{Var}[{\bf \beta|y,X,\sigma^2}]=(\Sigma_0^{-1}+{\bf X^TX/\sigma^2})^{-1}. \end{aligned} ​E[β∣y,X,σ2]=(Σ0−1​+XTX/σ2)−1(Σ0−1​β0​+XTy/σ2)Var[β∣y,X,σ2]=(Σ0−1​+XTX/σ2)−1.​

Suppose β0=0\beta_0=0β0​=0 and Σ0=α−1I\Sigma_0=\alpha^{-1}{\bf I}Σ0​=α−1I. The posterior density is simplified as

p(β∣y,X,σ2)∝exp⁡{−12(βT(αI+XTX/σ2)β−2βTXTy/σ2)}.p(\beta|{\bf y,X,\sigma^2})\propto \exp\{-\frac{1}{2}(\beta^T({\bf \alpha I+X^TX/\sigma^2})\beta-2\beta^T{\bf X^Ty}/\sigma^2)\}. p(β∣y,X,σ2)∝exp{−21​(βT(αI+XTX/σ2)β−2βTXTy/σ2)}.

Maximizing the posterior probability is equivalent to minimizing the error

1σ2∑i=1n(yi−βTxi)2+αβTβ,\frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^n(y_i-\beta^T{\bf x_i})^2+\alpha\beta^T\beta, σ21​i=1∑n​(yi​−βTxi​)2+αβTβ,

which leads to the regularized least square problem.

A Semiconjugate Prior for σ2\sigma^2σ2

Let γ=1σ2\gamma=\frac{1}{\sigma^2}γ=σ21​ be the precision. Suppose γ∼Gamma(ν0/2,νoσ02/2)\gamma\sim Gamma(\nu_0/2,\nu_o\sigma^2_0/2)γ∼Gamma(ν0​/2,νo​σ02​/2). The posterior probability for γ\gammaγ is

p(γ∣y,X,β)∝p(γ)p(y∣X,β,γ)∝(γν0/2−1exp⁡{−γν0σ0/2})×(γn/2exp⁡{−γSSR(β)/2})=γ(ν0+n)/2−1exp⁡{−γ(ν0σ02+SSR(β))/2}.\begin{aligned} &p(\gamma|{\bf y,X,\beta})\propto p(\gamma)p({\bf y}|{\bf X,\beta,\gamma})\\ \propto&(\gamma^{\nu_0/2-1}\exp\{-\gamma\nu_0\sigma^0/2\})\times(\gamma^{n/2}\exp\{-\gamma SSR(\beta)/2\})\\ =&\gamma^{(\nu_0+n)/2-1}\exp\{-\gamma(\nu_0\sigma^2_0+SSR(\beta))/2\}. \end{aligned} ∝=​p(γ∣y,X,β)∝p(γ)p(y∣X,β,γ)(γν0​/2−1exp{−γν0​σ0/2})×(γn/2exp{−γSSR(β)/2})γ(ν0​+n)/2−1exp{−γ(ν0​σ02​+SSR(β))/2}.​

Hence, the posterior distribution for γ\gammaγ is Gamma((ν0+n)/2,(ν0σ02+SSR(β))/2)Gamma((\nu_0+n)/2,(\nu_0\sigma_0^2+SSR(\beta))/2)Gamma((ν0​+n)/2,(ν0​σ02​+SSR(β))/2).

Gibbs Sampler

Given current values {β(s),(σ2)(s)}\{\beta^{(s)},(\sigma^2)^{(s)}\}{β(s),(σ2)(s)}. We can sample a new value by

  1. Updating β\betaβ:
    1. Compute E[β∣y,X,(σ2)(s)]\mathbb E[\beta|{\bf y,X,(\sigma^2)^{(s)}}]E[β∣y,X,(σ2)(s)] and Var[β∣y,X,(σ2)(s)]\textrm{Var}[\beta|{\bf y,X,(\sigma^2)^{(s)}}]Var[β∣y,X,(σ2)(s)];
    2. Sample β(s+1)\beta^{(s+1)}β(s+1) according to N(E,Var)\mathcal N(\mathbb E,\textrm{Var})N(E,Var).
  2. Updating σ2\sigma^2σ2:
    1. Compute SSR(β(s+1))SSR(\beta^{(s+1)})SSR(β(s+1));
    2. Sample (σ2)(s+1)(\sigma^2)^{(s+1)}(σ2)(s+1) according to inverse-gamma.

Zellner’s g-Prior

In the setting of prior distribution of β\betaβ, let β0=0\beta_0=0β0​=0 and Σ0=k(XTX)−1\Sigma_0=k({\bf X^TX})^{-1}Σ0​=k(XTX)−1, where k=gσ2k=g\sigma^2k=gσ2. We obtain what is called g-prior. The mean and variance are simplified as

E[β∣y,X,σ2]=gg+1σ2ΦVar[β∣y,X,σ2]=gg+1ΦXTy.\begin{aligned} &\mathbb E[\beta|{\bf y,X,\sigma^2}]=\frac{g}{g+1}\sigma^2{\bf \Phi}\\ &\textrm{Var}[\beta|{\bf y,X,\sigma^2}]=\frac{g}{g+1}{\bf \Phi X^Ty}. \end{aligned} ​E[β∣y,X,σ2]=g+1g​σ2ΦVar[β∣y,X,σ2]=g+1g​ΦXTy.​

where Φ=(XTX)−1\bf\Phi=(X^TX)^{-1}Φ=(XTX)−1. Suppose the prior distribution for σ2\sigma^2σ2 is inverse-gamma. We will show that p(σ2∣y,X)p(\sigma^2|{\bf y,X})p(σ2∣y,X) is inverse-gamma. Compared with the above discussion, this simplifies our sampling procedure since the posterior does NOT depend on β\betaβ.

Note that

p(y∣X,σ2)=∫p(y∣X,σ2,β)p(β∣X,σ2)dβ.p({\bf y}|{\bf X,\sigma^2})=\int p({\bf y}|{\bf X,\sigma^2,\beta})p(\beta|{\bf X,\sigma^2}){\rm d}\beta. p(y∣X,σ2)=∫p(y∣X,σ2,β)p(β∣X,σ2)dβ.

Substituting the densities, we obtain

p(y∣X,σ2,β)p(β∣X,σ2)=Cexp⁡{−12σ2(y−Xβ)T(y−Xβ)−12gσ2βTΦ−1β}.\begin{aligned} p({\bf y}|{\bf X,\sigma^2,\beta})p(\beta|{\bf X,\sigma^2})&=C \exp\{-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}({\bf y-X\beta})^T({\bf y-X\beta})-\frac{1}{2g\sigma^2}\beta^T{\bf \Phi^{-1}}\beta\}. \end{aligned} p(y∣X,σ2,β)p(β∣X,σ2)​=Cexp{−2σ21​(y−Xβ)T(y−Xβ)−2gσ21​βTΦ−1β}.​

where

C=(2πσ2)−n/2×∣2πgσ2Φ∣−1/2.C=(2\pi\sigma^2)^{-n/2}\times|2\pi g\sigma^2{\Phi}|^{-1/2}. C=(2πσ2)−n/2×∣2πgσ2Φ∣−1/2.

The terms in the exponent can be rewritten as

−12σ2yTy−12(β−m)TV−1(β−m)+12mTV−1m.-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}{\bf y^Ty}-\frac{1}{2}(\beta-m)^TV^{-1}(\beta-m)+\frac{1}{2}m^TV^{-1}m. −2σ21​yTy−21​(β−m)TV−1(β−m)+21​mTV−1m.

where

V=gg+1σ2Φm=gg+1ΦXTy.\begin{aligned} &V=\frac{g}{g+1}\sigma^2{\bf \Phi}\\ &m=\frac{g}{g+1}{\bf \Phi X^Ty}. \end{aligned} ​V=g+1g​σ2Φm=g+1g​ΦXTy.​

The integration is

p(y∣X,σ2)=(2π)−n/2(1+g)−p/2(σ2)−n/2exp⁡{−12σ2SSRg},p({\bf y}|{\bf X,\sigma^2})=(2\pi)^{-n/2}(1+g)^{-p/2}(\sigma^2)^{-n/2}\exp\{-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}SSR_g\}, p(y∣X,σ2)=(2π)−n/2(1+g)−p/2(σ2)−n/2exp{−2σ21​SSRg​},

where

SSRg=yT(I−gg+1XTΦX)y.SSR_g=\bf y^T(I-{\it \frac{g}{g+1}}X^T\Phi X)y. SSRg​=yT(I−g+1g​XTΦX)y.

Let γ=1/σ2∼Gamma(ν0/2,ν0σ02/2)\gamma=1/\sigma^2\sim Gamma(\nu_0/2,\nu_0\sigma^2_0/2)γ=1/σ2∼Gamma(ν0​/2,ν0​σ02​/2). Then we have

p(γ∣X,y)∝p(γ)p(y∣X,γ)∝γ(ν0+n)/2−1exp⁡{−γ(ν0σ02+SSRg)/2}.\begin{aligned} p(\gamma|{\bf X,y})&\propto p(\gamma)p({\bf y}|{\bf X,\gamma})\\ &\propto \gamma^{(\nu_0+n)/2-1}\exp\{-\gamma(\nu_0\sigma^2_0+SSR_g)/2\}. \end{aligned} p(γ∣X,y)​∝p(γ)p(y∣X,γ)∝γ(ν0​+n)/2−1exp{−γ(ν0​σ02​+SSRg​)/2}.​

Therefore, the posterior distribution for σ2\sigma^2σ2 is inverse-gamma with parameter ((ν0+n)/2,(ν0σ02+SSRg)/2)((\nu_0+n)/2,(\nu_0\sigma^2_0+SSR_g)/2)((ν0​+n)/2,(ν0​σ02​+SSRg​)/2).

Monte Carlo Sampler

Using g-prior, we see that the posterior distribution of σ2\sigma^2σ2 does not contain β\betaβ. Thus we can use a simple sampling procedure

  1. Sample σ2\sigma^2σ2 according to inverse-gamma;
  2. Sample β∼N(gg+1β^ols,gg+1σ2Φ)\beta\sim\mathcal N(\frac{g}{g+1}\hat{\beta}_{ols},\frac{g}{g+1}\sigma^2{\bf \Phi})β∼N(g+1g​β^​ols​,g+1g​σ2Φ).

Reference

  1. Hoff, Peter D. A first course in Bayesian statistical methods. Vol. 580. New York: Springer, 2009.
  2. Bishop, Christopher M. Pattern recognition and machine learning. springer, 2006.

entry.title: Notes on KNN Density Estimates (1)
entry.content:
This series is a note of Y. P. Mack and M. Rosenblatt’s work in 1979.

Introduction

Suppose we have a bounded, twice differentiable density function fff on Rp\mathbb R^pRp. Let X1,X2,⋯ ,XnX_1,X_2,\cdots,X_nX1​,X2​,⋯,Xn​ be nnn i.i.d. samples of fff. Let x∈Rpx\in\mathbb R^px∈Rp be a fixed point so that f(x)>0f(x)>0f(x)>0. We want to estimate f(x)f(x)f(x) based on nnn samples. The KNN estimate is given by

fn(x)=1n(Zn)p∑j=1nω(x−XjZn),f_n(x)=\frac{1}{n(Z_n)^p}\sum_{j=1}^n\omega(\frac{x-X_j}{Z_n}), fn​(x)=n(Zn​)p1​j=1∑n​ω(Zn​x−Xj​​),

where ZnZ_nZn​ is the distance between xxx and its kkkth nearest neighbor, and ω\omegaω is a bounded integrable weight function with ∫ω(u)du=1\int\omega(u){\rm d}u=1∫ω(u)du=1.

Let ω(u)=1∣B0(1)∣χB0(1)(u)\omega(u)=\frac{1}{|B_0(1)|}\chi_{B_0(1)}(u)ω(u)=∣B0​(1)∣1​χB0​(1)​(u), where B0(1)={x∈Rp:∥x∥≤1}B_0(1)=\{x\in\mathbb R^p:\|x\|\le 1\}B0​(1)={x∈Rp:∥x∥≤1} is the solid ball centered at origin with radius 1, and ∣B0(1)∣=πp/2Γ(p+22)|B_0(1)|=\frac{\pi^{p/2}}{\Gamma(\frac{p+2}{2})}∣B0​(1)∣=Γ(2p+2​)πp/2​ is the volume of unit ball. Under these settings we see that the KNN estimate is

fn(x)=1n(Zn)p⋅k∣B0(1)∣=kn⋅1∣B0(Zn)∣.f_n(x)=\frac{1}{n(Z_n)^p}\cdot\frac{k}{|B_0(1)|}=\frac{k}{n}\cdot\frac{1}{|B_0(Z_n)|}. fn​(x)=n(Zn​)p1​⋅∣B0​(1)∣k​=nk​⋅∣B0​(Zn​)∣1​.

If we see kn\frac{k}{n}nk​ as the probability that points lie in a ball around xxx, then it is a naive analogy of probability=∫density≈density×area\text{probability}=\int\text{density}\approx\text{density}\times \text{area}probability=∫density≈density×area. In the following we always assume that k=k(n)k=k(n)k=k(n) is a function of sample size nnn and k(n)→∞,k(n)/n→0k(n)\to \infty,k(n)/n\to0k(n)→∞,k(n)/n→0 as n→∞n\to\inftyn→∞.

We first investigate several properties of ZnZ_nZn​. For simplicity, we call ZnZ_nZn​ the KNN distance variable.

KNN distance variable ZnZ_nZn​

CDF and PDF of ZnZ_nZn​

For fixed x∈Rpx\in\mathbb R^px∈Rp, note that ZnZ_nZn​ is a function of nnn i.i.d. samples. Let H(r)H(r)H(r) be the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of ZnZ_nZn​. Let

G(r)=P(Bx(r))=∫Bx(r)f(u)du.G(r)=\mathbb P(B_x(r))=\int_{B_x(r)}f(u){\rm d}u. G(r)=P(Bx​(r))=∫Bx​(r)​f(u)du.

For any ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0, consider the probability H(r+ϵ)−H(r)=P(r≤Zn≤r+ϵ)H(r+\epsilon)-H(r)=\mathbb P(r\le Z_n\le r+\epsilon)H(r+ϵ)−H(r)=P(r≤Zn​≤r+ϵ). Suppose ϵ\epsilonϵ is small enough so that there is exactly one point lying in the shell of thickness ϵ\epsilonϵ (It happens when multiple points lie in the shell, but they will not contribute to our deduction since higher order of ϵ\epsilonϵ vanishes when taking limit). There are nnn choices of kkkth nearest point, (n−1k−1)\binom{n-1}{k-1}(k−1n−1​) choices of k−1k-1k−1 points lying within the rrr ball and others are outside the r+ϵr+\epsilonr+ϵ ball. Therefore, we have

H(r+ϵ)−H(r)=n(G(r+ϵ)−G(r))(n−1k−1)G(r)k−1(1−G(r+ϵ))n−k.H(r+\epsilon)-H(r)=n(G(r+\epsilon)-G(r))\binom{n-1}{k-1}G(r)^{k-1}(1-G(r+\epsilon))^{n-k}. H(r+ϵ)−H(r)=n(G(r+ϵ)−G(r))(k−1n−1​)G(r)k−1(1−G(r+ϵ))n−k.

Let h(r)h(r)h(r) be the probability density function (PDF) of ZnZ_nZn​. By definition, we have

h(r)=lim⁡ϵ→0H(r+ϵ)−H(r)ϵ=n(n−1k−1)G(r)k−1(1−G(r))n−kG′(r).h(r)=\lim\limits_{\epsilon\to0}\frac{H(r+\epsilon)-H(r)}{\epsilon}=n\binom{n-1}{k-1}G(r)^{k-1}(1-G(r))^{n-k}G'(r). h(r)=ϵ→0lim​ϵH(r+ϵ)−H(r)​=n(k−1n−1​)G(r)k−1(1−G(r))n−kG′(r).

Furthermore, G′(r)G'(r)G′(r) can be expressed as an integration of fff. Note that

G′(r)=lim⁡δ→01δ[∫Bx(r+δ)f(u)du−∫Bx(r)f(u)du]=lim⁡δ→01δ∫rr+δρp−1dρ∫Sx(1)f(ρ,t)dσ(t)=∫Sx(r)f(t)dσ(t),\begin{aligned} G'(r)&=\lim\limits_{\delta\to 0}\frac{1}{\delta}[\int_{B_x(r+\delta)}f(u){\rm d}u-\int_{B_x(r)}f(u){\rm d}u]\\ &=\lim_{\delta\to0}\frac{1}{\delta}\int_r^{r+\delta}\rho^{p-1}{\rm d}\rho\int_{S_x(1)}f(\rho,t){\rm d}\sigma(t)\\ &=\int_{S_x(r)}f(t){\rm d}\sigma(t), \end{aligned} G′(r)​=δ→0lim​δ1​[∫Bx​(r+δ)​f(u)du−∫Bx​(r)​f(u)du]=δ→0lim​δ1​∫rr+δ​ρp−1dρ∫Sx​(1)​f(ρ,t)dσ(t)=∫Sx​(r)​f(t)dσ(t),​

where Sx(r)={y∈Rp:∥y−x∥=r}S_x(r)=\{y\in\mathbb R^p:\|y-x\|=r\}Sx​(r)={y∈Rp:∥y−x∥=r} is the sphere centered at xxx with radius rrr, and dσ{\rm d}\sigmadσ is the volume element on the sphere.

On the other hand, for each XiX_iXi​, ∥Xi−x∥\|X_i-x\|∥Xi​−x∥ is a random variable with CDF G(r)G(r)G(r) and PDF G′(r)G'(r)G′(r). Note that ZnZ_nZn​ is exactly the kkkth order statistic from i.i.d. samples ∥Xi−x∥\|X_i-x\|∥Xi​−x∥. The kkkth order statistic gives the same h(r)h(r)h(r) as we deduced above.

Moments of ZnZ_nZn​

In general, for a measurable function ϕ\phiϕ, we want to compute the expectation Eϕ(Zn)\mathbb{E}\phi(Z_n)Eϕ(Zn​). By definition,

Eϕ(Zn)=∫0∞ϕ(r)h(r)dr=n(n−1k−1)∫0∞ϕ(r)G(r)k−1(1−G(r))n−kdG(r).\begin{aligned} \mathbb E\phi(Z_n)&=\int_0^{\infty}\phi(r)h(r){\rm d}r\\ &=n\binom{n-1}{k-1}\int_0^{\infty}\phi(r)G(r)^{k-1}(1-G(r))^{n-k}{\rm d}G(r). \end{aligned} Eϕ(Zn​)​=∫0∞​ϕ(r)h(r)dr=n(k−1n−1​)∫0∞​ϕ(r)G(r)k−1(1−G(r))n−kdG(r).​

In our situation, we consider a specific function of the type

ϕ(r)=rλG(r)γ(1−G(r))β,\phi(r)=\frac{r^{\lambda}}{G(r)^\gamma(1-G(r))^\beta}, ϕ(r)=G(r)γ(1−G(r))βrλ​,

where λ,γ,β\lambda,\gamma,\betaλ,γ,β are nonnegative integers. Note that G(r)∈[0,1]G(r)\in[0,1]G(r)∈[0,1]. To ensure that the integration exists, we assume that 1−G(r)=O(r−ξ)1-G(r)=O(r^{-\xi})1−G(r)=O(r−ξ), where ξ>0\xi>0ξ>0, as r→∞r\to \inftyr→∞.

Since G(r)G(r)G(r) is a monotonically increasing function, we can solve r=G−1(t)r=G^{-1}(t)r=G−1(t) where t∈[0,1]t\in[0,1]t∈[0,1]. On the one hand,

G(r)=∫Bx(r)f(u)du=f(x)∣Bx(r)∣+∫Bx(r)f(u)−f(x)du,\begin{aligned} G(r)&=\int_{B_x(r)}f(u){\rm d}u\\ &=f(x)|B_x(r)|+\int_{B_x(r)}f(u)-f(x){\rm d}u,\\ \end{aligned} G(r)​=∫Bx​(r)​f(u)du=f(x)∣Bx​(r)∣+∫Bx​(r)​f(u)−f(x)du,​

where

∫Bx(r)∣f(u)−f(x∣du≤K(r)∫Bx(r)∥u−x∥du=K(r)n+1rp+1∣Sx(1)∣.\int_{B_x(r)}|f(u)-f(x|{\rm d}u\le K(r)\int_{B_x(r)}\|u-x\|{\rm d }u=\frac{K(r)}{n+1}r^{p+1}|S_x(1)|. ∫Bx​(r)​∣f(u)−f(x∣du≤K(r)∫Bx​(r)​∥u−x∥du=n+1K(r)​rp+1∣Sx​(1)∣.

Therefore, t=G(r)=cf(x)rp+o(rp)t=G(r)=cf(x)r^p+o(r^p)t=G(r)=cf(x)rp+o(rp) as r→0r\to 0r→0, where c=∣Bx(1)∣c=|B_x(1)|c=∣Bx​(1)∣. From the assumption 1−G(r)=O(r−ξ)1-G(r)=O(r^{-\xi})1−G(r)=O(r−ξ), we see that t=1−O(r−ξ)t=1-O(r^{-\xi})t=1−O(r−ξ) as r→∞r\to\inftyr→∞. We can write rrr as

r=G−1(t)=(tcf(x))1/p+η(t),r=G^{-1}(t)=(\frac{t}{cf(x)})^{1/p}+\eta(t), r=G−1(t)=(cf(x)t​)1/p+η(t),

where η(t)=o(t1/p)\eta(t)=o(t^{1/p})η(t)=o(t1/p) as t→0t\to 0t→0 and η(t)=O(1(1−t)ξ)\eta(t)=O(\frac{1}{(1-t)^\xi})η(t)=O((1−t)ξ1​) as t→1t\to 1t→1.

Using the change of variable t=G(r)t=G(r)t=G(r), we see that

Eϕ(Zn)=n(n−1k−1)∫01(tcf(x)+η(t))λtk−1−γ(1−t)n−k−βdt\begin{aligned} \mathbb E\phi(Z_n)&=n\binom{n-1}{k-1}\int_0^1(\frac{t}{cf(x)}+\eta(t))^\lambda t^{k-1-\gamma}(1-t)^{n-k-\beta}{\rm d}t\\ \end{aligned} Eϕ(Zn​)​=n(k−1n−1​)∫01​(cf(x)t​+η(t))λtk−1−γ(1−t)n−k−βdt​

Let QQQ be the kkkth nearest neighbor, Y1,Y2,⋯ ,Yk−1Y_1,Y_2,\cdots,Y_{k-1}Y1​,Y2​,⋯,Yk−1​ be the k−1k-1k−1 points lying within Bx(Zn)=Bx(∥Q−x∥)B_x(Z_n)=B_x(\|Q-x\|)Bx​(Zn​)=Bx​(∥Q−x∥), and V1,V2,⋯ ,Vn−kV_1,V_2,\cdots,V_{n-k}V1​,V2​,⋯,Vn−k​ be n−kn-kn−k points falling outside Bx(Zn)B_x(Z_n)Bx​(Zn​). Suppose Zn=rZ_n=rZn​=r is known. Then Q,YQ,YQ,Y and VVV will be independent. The conditional densities are

f(yi∣r)=f(yi)G(r), yi∈B˚x(r)f(vi∣r)=f(vi)1−G(r), vi∈(Bx(r))cf(q∣r)=f(q)G′(r), q∈Sx(r).\begin{aligned} & f(y_i|r)=\frac{f(y_i)}{G(r)},\, y_i\in \mathring B_x(r)\\ & f(v_i|r)=\frac{f(v_i)}{1-G(r)},\, v_i\in (B_x(r))^c\\ & f(q|r)=\frac{f(q)}{G'(r)}, \, q\in S_x(r). \end{aligned} ​f(yi​∣r)=G(r)f(yi​)​,yi​∈B˚x​(r)f(vi​∣r)=1−G(r)f(vi​)​,vi​∈(Bx​(r))cf(q∣r)=G′(r)f(q)​,q∈Sx​(r).​

Asymptotic moments of near–neighbour distance distributions

entry.title: A Proof of 'Everything is connected'
entry.content:
Stories

The idea about building my own blog was originated from a long time ago when I first read Terry Tao’s homepage. In 2019, when I became a graduate student, I realized it is necessary to write down the mathematics to keep track of my research. Honestly speaking, repetitions and failures really make one suffer. It is those tiny progresses that encourage me to hold on.

The title of this blog comes from an essay which ingeniously describes a philosophy, that every thing, every event, every people are connected with each other. Like da Vinci said, Learn how to see. Realize that everything connects to everything else. When I read these, a great idea hit me that I could formulate a mathematical proposition and prove it. Though the proof seems naive, I was happy to find such an explanation.

Modeling

Let us recall some basics from probabilistic graphical model. We use random variables to represent true events in real life. The relationship between events is modeled by conditional independence. Suppose X,YX,YX,Y and ZZZ are three random variables. If the joint probability density function of X,YX,YX,Y and ZZZ satisfy the following equation

f(x,y∣z)=f(x∣z)f(y∣z),f(x,y|z)=f(x|z)f(y|z), f(x,y∣z)=f(x∣z)f(y∣z),

XXX and YYY are said to be conditional independent given ZZZ. It generalizes the concept of independence where we known nothing prior (Z=∅Z=\emptysetZ=∅). In our case, it is reasonable that two events have no relationship if they are independent when we know all the other things.

Now we can construct a graph based on the above argument. The vertices are random variables. Two distinct vertices are connected by an edge if they are not conditional independent. One event is ‘connected’ to another if there is a path joining them. Therefore, when we say ‘Everything is connected’, we actually mean that the graph is connected.

However, we should be cautious about ‘everything’ since we just considered finite number of events. Given arbitrary nnn events, we can say ‘These nnn things are connected’ if the corresponding graph is connected. But it is too stupid to say such things, and we are not going to infer whether a graph is connected, either. Instead, let us look at the probability that a graph with nnn nodes is connected.

Proof

Let Gn\mathbb{G}_nGn​ be the space of undirected simple graphs with nnn nodes. Gn\mathbb{G}_nGn​ is a finite set with 2n(n−1)22^\frac{n(n-1)}{2}22n(n−1)​ elements. Let Gnc\mathbb{G}_n^cGnc​ be the subset of all connected graphs, Gnd\mathbb G_n^dGnd​ be the subset of all disconnected graphs. Define the connectivity ratio

Rn=#(Gnd)#(Gn).R_n=\frac{ \#(\mathbb{G}_n^d)}{ \#(\mathbb{G}_n)}. Rn​=#(Gn​)#(Gnd​)​.

Denote #(Gnc)\#(\mathbb G_n^c)#(Gnc​) by KncK_n^cKnc​ and #(Gnd)\#(\mathbb G_n^d)#(Gnd​) by KndK_n^dKnd​. We have Knc+Knd=2n(n−1)2K_n^c+K_n^d=2^\frac{n(n-1)}{2}Knc​+Knd​=22n(n−1)​.

Let v0,v1,⋯ ,vnv_0,v_1,\cdots,v_nv0​,v1​,⋯,vn​ be vertices of a graph G∈Gn+1dG\in\mathbb G_{n+1}^dG∈Gn+1d​. Suppose C0C_0C0​ is the component containing v0v_0v0​ with iii points. Let C1C_1C1​ be the complement of C0C_0C0​ with n+1−in+1-in+1−i points. The we have the following recursion

Kn+1d=∑i=1n(ni−1)Kic2(n+1−i)(n−i)2.K_{n+1}^d=\sum_{i=1}^n\binom{n}{i-1}K_i^c2^\frac{(n+1-i)(n-i)}{2}. Kn+1d​=i=1∑n​(i−1n​)Kic​22(n+1−i)(n−i)​.

Dividing both sides by 2(n+1)n22^\frac{(n+1)n}{2}22(n+1)n​, we have

Rn+1=∑i=1n(ni−1)(1−Ri)2−i(n+1−i)2.R_{n+1}=\sum_{i=1}^n\binom{n}{i-1}(1-R_i)2^\frac{-i(n+1-i)}{2}. Rn+1​=i=1∑n​(i−1n​)(1−Ri​)22−i(n+1−i)​.

Note that 1−Ri<11-R_i<11−Ri​<1 when i>1i>1i>1. Multiply both sides by 2n2^n2n,

2nRn+1<1+n+∑i=2n−1(ni−1)2−(n−i)(i−1).2^nR_{n+1}<1+n+\sum_{i=2}^{n-1}\binom{n}{i-1}2^{-(n-i)(i-1)}. 2nRn+1​<1+n+i=2∑n−1​(i−1n​)2−(n−i)(i−1).

When 1<i<n1<i<n1<i<n, 2−(n−i)(i−1)<2−(n−2)2^{-(n-i)(i-1)}<2^{-(n-2)}2−(n−i)(i−1)<2−(n−2). We obtain that 2nRn+1<n+52^nR_{n+1}<n+52nRn+1​<n+5. That is, the connectivity ratio Rn<n+42n−1R_n<\frac{n+4}{2^{n-1}}Rn​<2n−1n+4​. When nnn tends to infinity, RnR_nRn​ tends to 000.

Given arbitrarily nnn events, we are right to guess that they are connected with probability greater than 1−n+42n−11-\frac{n+4}{2^{n-1}}1−2n−1n+4​. The probability grows with respect to the number of events. When nnn goes to infinity, we are 100% certain that ‘Everything is connected’!
